examples.title: A* Search Algorithm
examples.description: Standard A* graph search with admissible heuristic
examples.code:
FUNCTION A-Star(start, goal, h):
 OPEN ← priority queue ordered by f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
 CLOSED ← empty set
 g(start) ← 0
 f(start) ← h(start)
 INSERT(start, OPEN)
 
 WHILE OPEN is not empty:
 current ← node with lowest f in OPEN
 
 IF current = goal:
 RETURN PATH(current) // Optimal path found
 
 REMOVE current FROM OPEN
 ADD current TO CLOSED
 
 FOR each successor of current:
 tentative_g ← g(current) + cost(current, successor)
 
 IF successor in CLOSED:
 CONTINUE
 
 IF successor not in OPEN OR tentative_g < g(successor):
 g(successor) ← tentative_g
 f(successor) ← g(successor) + h(successor)
 parent(successor) ← current
 
 IF successor not in OPEN:
 INSERT(successor, OPEN)
 
 RETURN FAILURE // No solution exists
examples.explanation: A* maintains OPEN (frontier) and CLOSED (explored) sets. Nodes are expanded in order of f(n)=g(n)+h(n). With admissible h, first goal reached gives optimal solution. Graph search version ensures no cycles via CLOSED.

examples.title: Greedy Best-First Search
examples.description: Informed search using only heuristic values
examples.code:
FUNCTION Greedy-Best-First-Search(start, goal, h):
 OPEN ← priority queue ordered by h(n) only
 CLOSED ← empty set
 
 INSERT(start, OPEN)
 
 WHILE OPEN is not empty:
 current ← node with lowest h in OPEN
 
 IF current = goal:
 RETURN PATH(current)
 
 REMOVE current FROM OPEN
 ADD current TO CLOSED
 
 FOR each successor of current:
 IF successor in CLOSED:
 CONTINUE
 parent(successor) ← current
 INSERT(successor, OPEN)
 
 RETURN FAILURE
examples.explanation: Greedy Best-First uses only h(n) for ordering, ignoring actual cost g(n) from start. This makes it fast but not guaranteed optimal. Can get stuck in deep non-optimal paths. Similar structure to A* but uses h alone for priority.

examples.title: Iterative Deepening A* (IDA*)
examples.description: Memory-efficient A* using iterative deepening on f-cost
examples.code:
FUNCTION IDA-Star(start, goal, h):
 threshold ← h(start)
 
 WHILE true:
 result ← Recursive-Search(start, goal, h, threshold)
 
 IF result = FOUND:
 RETURN path
 IF result = ∞: // No solution within threshold
 RETURN FAILURE
 
 threshold ← result // Next threshold is minimum f exceeding current

FUNCTION Recursive-Search(node, goal, h, threshold):
 f ← g(node) + h(node)
 
 IF f > threshold:
 RETURN f // Return f-value as next threshold candidate
 
 IF node = goal:
 RETURN FOUND
 
 min ← ∞
 
 FOR each successor of node:
 temp ← Recursive-Search(successor, goal, h, threshold)
 
 IF temp = FOUND:
 RETURN FOUND
 IF temp < min:
 min ← temp
 
 RETURN min
examples.explanation: IDA* performs depth-first search with f-cost bound (threshold). Starts with h(start), iteratively increases threshold to next highest f-value encountered. Uses only O(bd) memory (path length) but may explore same nodes multiple times like Iterative Deepening DFS.
examples.timeComplexity: O(b^d) but with higher constant than A*

examples.title: Manhattan Distance Heuristic for 8-Puzzle
examples.description: Example of admissible heuristic derivation from relaxed problem
examples.explanation: Manhattan distance is derived from the 8-puzzle relaxed problem where tiles can move directly to their goal positions ignoring other tiles. It is admissible because actual moves require going around obstacles, so real cost ≥ Manhattan distance. For 8-puzzle, this dominates the simpler 'misplaced tiles' heuristic.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of tiles
